schema.name: Name
schema.Description: Full name is computed from the first and last name of the customer

schema.name: FirstName
schema.Description: First Name of the customer - used to make the full name

schema.name: LastName
schema.Description: Last Name of the customer - used to make the full name

schema.name: MetaKey
schema.Description: The metadata key (e.g. 'tagline', 'motif_palette', 'substrates'). Unique within the table.

schema.name: Name
schema.Description: Identifier for this metadata entry. Mirrors MetaKey so the row is addressable by Name like every other table.

schema.name: ValueType
schema.Description: How to interpret the value columns: 'string' (use StringValue), 'object' (parse JsonValue as JSON object), 'array' (parse JsonValue as JSON array).

schema.name: StringValue
schema.Description: Plain string value. Populated when ValueType == 'string'; null otherwise.

schema.name: JsonValue
schema.Description: JSON-encoded value. Populated when ValueType == 'object' or 'array'; null when ValueType == 'string'.

data.StringValue: One table. One calculated field. That's the whole demo.

The smallest non-trivial Effortless rulebook. **One** entity (`Customers`), three rows, and a single calculated field — `FullName = FirstName & " " & LastName`. The argument is the simplicity itself: this much logic, lifted out of application code and into the rulebook, propagates to every substrate. Postgres view, Python class, Excel cell, OWL ontology — all four compute the same answer the same way, from the same rulebook JSON, with zero glue code.

This is the rulebook to point a skeptic at when *every other demo feels like too much*.

data.JsonValue: ["**Edit `FirstName` on any row and watch `FullName` recompute in every substrate.** No application code reads this field — the rulebook IS the field.", "**Compare the Postgres view, the Python class, and the Excel cell** for the same row. They all compute `FullName` from the same formula declared once in the rulebook.", "**Try adding a calculated field of your own** — say `Initials = LEFT(FirstName, 1) & LEFT(LastName, 1)`. Run `effortless build`. Every substrate now has it."]

data.StringValue: Three customers. One calculated field. The whole point of ERB, on a napkin.
